content.full_text:
June 29, 1976

Assemblyman Mike D. Antonovich
Assembly Post Office
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Assemblyman Antonovich:

Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director of the Commission,
referred your inquiry of June 16, 1976 to me for response.

You stated that you received from an unknown source a certificate for 100 shares of capital stock in Thommen Incorporated
which you do not wish to keep. You asked how to avoid reporting
responsibility or legal liability under the Political Reform Act.

The New York Secretary of State's office has information
regarding two companies by the name of Thommen, Inc. One of the
companies was incorporated in New York on June 13, 1966, but was
dissolved by proclamation of the state on December 15, 1970 for
failure to pay franchise taxes. The address of the company at
the time of incorporation was 101 Park Avenue, New York, New York.
The Secretary of State also has records of a Thommen, Inc., formerly known as Thommen Sports Equipment, 28 East 22nd Street, New
York, New York, which dissolved in 1961. Although the companies
may still be in business, the Secretary of State has no record of
an existing corporation by the name of Thommen, Inc. The New York
City telephone directory lists a Thommen, Inc. at 298 - 5th Avenue,
212/239-8422. If by writing Thommen Inc. at the above addresses
you are able to ascertain which company issued certificate no. 133
on June 2, 1976, you could avoid reporting receipt of the stock
by simply returning it to that company.

Alternatively, there are two methods of disposing of the stock
under the Act, depending upon whether you consider it a gift or a
campaign contribution.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215, enclosed, defines a "contribution" as any nonmonetary payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is received by a candidate,
unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was received for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy
or status as an officeholder. A "gift," as defined in Government


- 2 -

Code Section 82028, is any payment to the extent that considera-
tion of equal or greater value is not received.

Government Code Section 84303 prohibits a candidate, office-
holder or committee from keeping an anonymous contribution valued
at $50 or more and requires the recipient to turn such a contri-
bution over to the Secretary of State for deposit in the General
Fund of the state. Although you do not know whether the value of
the stock is over or under $50, you would not be required to report
its receipt under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Act if
you turned it over to the Secretary of State.

If you wish to consider the stock a gift, you will not be re-
quired to report receipt of it, within 30 days of its receipt,
you deliver it to a charitable organization without claiming it as
a charitable contribution for tax purposes. Government Code Sec-
tion 82020(b)(4).

Therefore, you will not be required to report receipt of the
stock if you return it, turn it over to the Secretary of State
for deposit in the general fund or deliver it to a charitable
organization. On the other hand, if you determine that the stock
has no value, you of course are not required by the Political
Reform Act to report its receipt or disposition.

Sincerely,


Nan Hambleton
Special Compliance Representative

NH:bw

Enclosure

bcc: Jeanne P.


[The document appears to be mostly blank with only mounting holes visible]

CAPITOL OFFICE
ASSEMBLY POST OFFICE
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814

DISTRICT OFFICE
512 EAST WILSON AVENUE
SUITE 210
GLENDALE 91205
(213) 240-6320

[SEAL]

COMMITTEES
EDUCATION
LABOR RELATIONS
TRANSPORTATION

Assembly
California Legislature

MIKE D. ANTONOVICH
ASSEMBLYMAN, FORTY-FIRST DISTRICT
MINORITY WHIP

June 16, 1976

Mr. Michael Bennett, Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Bennett:

A member of my staff found under my district office door
a certificate for 100 shares of capital stock in Thommen Incorporated made out in my name. A copy of the certificate is
enclosed.

I have no desire to keep the certificate and have no idea
who delivered the certificate or whether the certificate is
genuine. At the same time, I am reluctant to expend my resources
to determine the certificate's nature and source.

In view of possible reporting responsibilities under the
financial disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
of 1974 and possible liability under Section 86204 relating
to gifts by lobbyists, I seek your advice or a commission
opinion on any course of conduct I should undertake to avoid
any reporting responsibility or legal liability under the act.

Sincerely,

Mike D. Antonovich
MIKE D. ANTONOVICH

MDA:jc

Common Stock of

THOMSON INC.

transferable only on the books of the Corporation by the holder hereof in person or by duly authorized attorney, upon surrender of this certificate properly endorsed.


For Value Received _____ hereby sell, assign and transfer
unto _____ _____ Shares
of the Capital Stock represented by the within
Certificate, and do hereby irrevocably constitute and appoint _____ Attorney
to transfer the said Stock on the books of the within named
Corporation with full power of substitution in the premises.
Dated _____ 19_____
In presence of _____


Since the document is essentially blank or the text is too faint to read reliably, I cannot provide a meaningful transcription that would follow your instruction to copy text verbatim.
